confidence.disclosure: Khadija is one of the most important women in Islamic history, but the documentation of her life is sparse and almost entirely tied to the biography of the Prophet Muhammad. Most events are dated only by year and location. The early sira works of Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Sa'd provide most of what is known: her trading caravans, her two earlier marriages, her marriage to Muhammad, the births of her children, and her death three years before the Hijra. Specific dates within years are almost never given, and even her birth year (commonly given as 555 CE) is a reconstruction. The journey is therefore mostly stationary in Mecca, with the trading caravan to Syria as the only major travel event.

sources: Ibn Sa'd, Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir, vol. 8 (Women)

journey.event: Born in Mecca, in the Quraysh tribe, into the wealthy clan of Asad ibn Abd al-Uzza. Her father Khuwaylid is a respected Qurashi merchant. The year 555 CE is a common reconstruction; some sources give an earlier date.
Marries her first husband, Abu Hala al-Tamimi, with whom she has at least one son, Hind. He dies young. Sources differ on the exact order and identity of her early husbands.

After Abu Hala's death, marries Atiq ibn Aidh al-Makhzumi. This marriage also ends with his death, leaving her a widow for the second time. From these two marriages she inherits and accumulates substantial wealth.

Now twice widowed, becomes one of the most successful merchants in Mecca, hiring agents to take her caravans north to Syria and south to Yemen. Known by the titles al-Tahira (the Pure) and Sayyidat Quraysh (Lady of the Quraysh). She does not personally accompany her caravans.
Hires the young Muhammad ibn Abdullah, then about 25 and known as al-Amin (the Trustworthy), to take her trading caravan to Syria as her agent. Sends her servant Maysara along to assist him.

Caravan reaches Bostra in southern Syria. Muhammad sells the goods at unusual profit. Maysara reports back to Khadija the unusual conduct and integrity of the young man, including the story of the monk who recognised signs of prophethood in him.

Impressed by Muhammad's character and the report of Maysara, Khadija proposes marriage to him through her friend Nafisa bint Munyah. Muhammad accepts. They marry in Mecca, with a dowry of twenty young camels paid by Muhammad's uncle. She is about 40, he is about 25.

Bears Muhammad's first son Qasim, who dies in infancy. From this Muhammad takes the kunya Abu al-Qasim. Over the following years she also bears Zaynab, Ruqayyah, Umm Kulthum, Fatimah and (according to some sources) a second son Abdullah, who also dies young. Of these, only the four daughters survive to adulthood.

Muhammad returns from his retreat in the cave of Hira shaking with fear, after his first encounter with the angel Gabriel. Khadija wraps him in a cloak, calms him, and tells him: 'By Allah, Allah will never disgrace you. You join ties of relationship, you bear the burden of the weak, you help the destitute, you honour the guest, and you help against the calamities of fate.' She becomes the first person to believe in his prophethood.
journey.source: Sahih al-Bukhari, Bad' al-Wahy; Ibn Ishaq

journey.event: Takes Muhammad to her cousin Waraqa ibn Nawfal, an elderly Christian, who confirms that what came to him is the same Namus that came to Moses. Khadija becomes the first Muslim, embracing Islam before any other man, woman, or child.

When Muhammad goes public with his message, Khadija remains his strongest support. She uses her wealth to free enslaved Muslims and to provide for the poor early believers. Her status in Quraysh gives Muhammad some protection from the worst of the early persecution.

When Quraysh impose a social and economic boycott against the clan of Banu Hashim, Khadija enters the besieged quarter (Shi'b Abi Talib) with Muhammad and the other Hashimites. She spends most of her remaining wealth feeding the besieged, and her health is permanently damaged by hunger and hardship.

The boycott ends after about three years, but Khadija is by now seriously weakened. A few weeks after Muhammad's uncle Abu Talib dies, Khadija also passes away in Mecca, in the tenth year of prophethood, three years before the Hijra. Muhammad is devastated; the year becomes known as the Year of Sorrow ('Am al-Huzn). She is buried in the Hujun cemetery in Mecca. She had been Muhammad's only wife for the entire 25 years of their marriage.
